title: Trachymene hookeri
taxonomy: Trachymene hookeri is classified as a species of flowering plant in the carrot family, Apiaceae. It is native to Australia and is also known as the coastal benito or Hook's benito. This plant is further classified within the genus Trachymene and the family Apiaceae, with its full classification being Trachymene hookeri. The species is also sometimes referred to as Torilis hookeri. Trachymene hookeri is native to the eastern coast of Australia, particularly in New South Wales and Queensland.
uses: Trachymene hookeri, also known as coastal lily, is a plant with a long history of traditional use in Western Australia. The plant has been used by Indigenous Australians for various medicinal purposes, including the treatment of skin conditions and wounds. The roots of the plant are said to have anti-inflammatory properties, which may contribute to its potential medicinal uses. However, further research is needed to confirm the plant's efficacy and safety for human consumption. The plant's traditional uses highlight the importance of preserving indigenous knowledge and exploring the potential of native Australian flora.
benefits: Trachymene hookeri has been traditionally used in Australian herbal medicine to help alleviate cold and flu symptoms. Its leaves and roots contain compounds with anti-inflammatory properties. The plant is also believed to have antimicrobial and antiviral effects. However, further research is needed to confirm its efficacy in treating various health conditions. Trachymene hookeri is not widely studied as a medicinal plant, and its health benefits may vary depending on the source and method of preparation.

side_effects: Trachymene hookeri also known as the Yellow Button or the Common Trachymene, its flowers and young leaves can cause skin irritation and allergic reactions in some individuals. Handling the plant may lead to redness, itching, and even blistering in sensitive people. The sap of the plant contains toxic compounds that can cause irritation and discomfort. Ingestion of any part of the plant is not recommended as it may cause gastrointestinal problems and other adverse effects. People handling the plant should wear protective gloves and clothing to minimize exposure.
